title: Lesson 13: Lists in HTML (ul, ol, li)

content.explanation:
📋 Organizing Information Clearly

When websites display multiple items—like menus, features, or steps—they use lists.

Lists help structure content so it is easy to read, scan, and understand.

📌 Two Types of Lists

  • Unordered List (<ul>): Bullet points (no order)
  • Ordered List (<ol>): Numbered steps (sequence matters)

Each item inside a list uses the <li> tag.
content.syntax_breakdown:
🧪 List Structure

Unordered list example:

<ul>
  <li>HTML</li>
  <li>CSS</li>
  <li>JavaScript</li>
</ul>

Ordered list example:

<ol>
  <li>Step 1</li>
  <li>Step 2</li>
  <li>Step 3</li>
</ol>

🧠 Key Rule

👉 Only <li> elements are allowed inside lists.

🎯 Mission: Build your first list

1. Add an unordered list (<ul>) below the paragraph
2. Add 3 list items (<li>):

   - CSS
   - JavaScript
3. Run your code and observe bullet points

👉 Example:

Remember: every item must be inside
  • tags

quiz_engine.question: Which tag is used to create a list item in HTML?
quiz_engine.explanation_feedback: Correct 🎉 The <li> tag is used to define each item inside both ordered and unordered lists.

takeaways: Lists help organize multiple items clearly
Lists are used in menus, steps, and feature sections

cheat_sheet.li: List item inside a list container
